skill.name: ppt
skill.description: A code-first PPTX presentation generator. Define slide decks as JavaScript arrays with reusable components (comparison, timeline, stats, quote), preview as HTML in a browser, then export to PowerPoint PPTX format. Supports custom themes, animations, and iterative editing without PowerPoint.
skill.summary: Generate PPTX presentations from JavaScript slide definitions with HTML preview and export pipeline.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: The skill is a local presentation creation tool that converts JavaScript slide definitions to HTML previews and PPTX files. All static analysis findings are false positives: 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags CSS hex color values and unicode text, 'external_commands' flags markdown documentation code blocks and JavaScript template literals, 'obfuscation' flags Chinese text content and standard JS patterns, and 'certificate/key files' flags animation preset names. The innerHTML usage is appropriate for a local browser presentation tool where users provide their own content. No evidence of malicious intent, data exfiltration, or credential theft was found.

medium_findings.title: innerHTML usage in rendering engine (XSS risk)
medium_findings.description: The presentation engine uses innerHTML to render slide content from JavaScript data. Since this is a local tool and users provide their own slide content, the risk is low. However, if slide data contains user-supplied text with embedded HTML/JavaScript, it would execute in the browser preview. Consider using textContent or DOM manipulation methods for text content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Pattern found but context is a local presentation tool, not a multi-user web application. Users supply their own slide data so the XSS vector only affects the author themselves.

low_findings.title: Network access via urllib
low_findings.description: The converter module imports urllib.request for network requests. Review shows this is used for fetching external resources during PPTX conversion (such as images). No evidence of data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed legitimate use. The urllib import is used for fetching images and external resources during PPTX export as expected for a presentation tool.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs in converter module
low_findings.description: Several hardcoded URLs found in animation.py, components.py, and parsers.py. These are used for CSS/JS resource references in the HTML-to-PPTX conversion pipeline, not for data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: URL references are to local/resource paths used in HTML rendering context. No external domains that would suggest exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Environment variable access for configuration
low_findings.description: The converter reads environment variables for configuration. Review shows these are used for presentation settings (colors, layout parameters), not for credential access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Environment access pattern confirmed. Used for reading CSS variable overrides from environment, which is a legitimate configuration approach. No DATABASE_URL or credential-like env vars accessed.

low_findings.title: Unicode escape sequences in data files
low_findings.description: Unicode escape sequences found in fallback slide data and character replacement maps. These are Chinese text content properly escaped and character sanitization mappings, not obfuscation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed as legitimate Chinese text content in fallback slide data. The unicode escapes are JSON-encoded Chinese characters for a demo presentation about AI strategy.

content.value_statement: Creating professional slide decks often requires manual work in PowerPoint or Google Slides, which is slow and hard to version-control. This skill lets you define presentations as JavaScript data with reusable visual components, preview them as HTML, and export to PPTX format with consistent styling and animations.
actual_capabilities: Generate complete PPTX slide decks from JavaScript data definitions using reusable components like comparison, timeline, stats, quote, and valueCards
Preview presentations as interactive HTML in a browser before exporting to PPTX format
Support custom themes with configurable colors, fonts, and CSS variables across the deck
Include animation presets (fadeIn, slideUp, scaleIn, zoomIn, flipIn) with configurable duration and delay
Validate slide data for compatibility issues before conversion with a dedicated validation script
limitations: Requires Python 3.10+ and python-pptx library installed locally for PPTX export
Does not import existing PowerPoint templates or preserve PPSX/POTX formats
Animation effects in PPTX output may differ from HTML browser preview behavior
Complex slide layouts with many elements may need manual spacing adjustments

use_cases.title: Quick Pitch Deck from Outline
use_cases.description: Turn a rough topic outline into a visual slide deck with comparison slides, stats, and quote components. Iterate on content in code and export a final PPTX file.
use_cases.target_user: Product managers and startup founders creating pitch decks

use_cases.title: Technical Presentation with Code Blocks
use_cases.description: Build technical talks using the terminal component for code snippets, timeline for roadmap slides, and ending slides for call-to-action sections.
use_cases.target_user: Engineers and developer advocates preparing conference talks

use_cases.title: Standardized Report Templates
use_cases.description: Create reusable slide templates for weekly reports or client updates. Define the structure once in JavaScript and populate with fresh data each cycle.
use_cases.target_user: Consultants and analysts generating recurring client reports

prompt_templates.title: Generate a new 10-slide deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a presentation about [topic] with 10 slides. Use a mix of comparison, timeline, stats, and quote components. Use this project structure: each slide needs badge, title, subtitle, clickHint, and elements with step sequencing.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new presentation from scratch

prompt_templates.title: Add a timeline component to existing deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Add a timeline slide to the existing deck in projects/my-deck.js. The timeline should have 4 milestones: Q1 Planning, Q2 Development, Q3 Testing, Q4 Launch. Each milestone needs a title and description.

prompt_templates.title: Fix data validation issues
prompt_templates.prompt: Run validate_data.py on my project file projects/my-deck.js. If issues are found, use --fix to repair them. Show me what was changed.

prompt_templates.title: Generate both HTML preview and PPTX export
prompt_templates.prompt: Build the HTML preview for projects/my-deck.js with output name 'my-deck-preview', then convert the same project file to PPTX with output name 'my-deck-final'. Confirm both files were created in the output/ directory.

output_examples.input: Create a 6-slide presentation about renewable energy trends with comparison and stats components
output: Each slide has badge, title, subtitle, and component elements

output_examples.input: Add a timeline slide showing Q1-Q4 product milestones to the existing deck
output: Added timeline component with 4 milestones to projects/quarterly-review.js
Each milestone has step sequencing with slideUp animation

best_practices: Outline each slide with a single clear intent and express content as concise bullets or structured blocks before writing component definitions.
Choose one dominant color, two supporting colors, and one accent color for the palette to ensure visual consistency across the deck.
Include at least one visual component (comparison, stats, timeline, quote) on every slide to avoid text-only layouts.
anti_patterns: Do not embed HTML markup or raw JSX inside slide text fields. The framework expects plain text content for components.
Do not use the skill when the required source data, project files, or dependencies are missing. Validate inputs before execution.
Do not fabricate measurements, references, or conclusions not supported by the provided source material when generating presentation content.

faq.question: What Python packages do I need for PPTX export?
faq.answer: You need python-pptx and lxml packages. Install them with: pip install python-pptx lxml

faq.question: Can I import an existing PowerPoint file as a template?
faq.answer: No. This skill creates presentations from JavaScript data definitions only. It does not import existing PPTX, PPSX, or POTX files.

faq.question: Where are the generated files saved?
faq.answer: Generated HTML and PPTX files are saved to the output/ directory in the project root folder.

faq.question: What slide components are available?
faq.answer: Available components are: comparison, timeline, stats, valueCards, quote, ending, terminal, assumptions, competitionBox, and strategyBox.

faq.question: How do I customize the look of my presentation?
faq.answer: Edit the theme variables in framework/themes/default/variables.css. You can change colors, fonts, spacing, and other visual properties.

faq.question: Does the PPTX export preserve animations from the HTML preview?
faq.answer: Animations in PPTX output may differ from the HTML preview. The converter maps HTML animations to equivalent PowerPoint animations when possible.
